title: How to Develop Remote Viewing Skills
meta_description: Learn remote viewing as a learnable skill not a gift. Discover the structured protocol used by the Stargate Project to access information beyond normal senses.
og_title: How to Develop Remote Viewing Skills
og_description: Learn remote viewing as a learnable skill not a gift. Discover the structured protocol used by the Stargate Project to access information beyond normal senses.
body:
What Remote Viewing Actually Is (And Isn't)

Let's clear something up right now. Remote viewing isn't about seeing things with your eyes closed like a movie playing behind your eyelids. It's not about being psychic in the way you've seen in movies where someone describes a crime scene with perfect detail. And it's definitely not about being "special" or "gifted" in some way that you're not.

Remote viewing is a structured protocol for accessing information that your normal senses can't reach. It's a skill. Like learning to play piano or cook a decent meal. Some people pick it up faster than others, but anyone can learn it. The government spent millions of dollars researching this at Stanford Research Institute and later at Fort Meade. They called it the Stargate Project. And they proved that remote viewing works - not because the viewers were special, but because the protocol was solid.

You already have this ability. You've probably experienced it dozens of times without knowing what to call it. That sudden thought about an old friend right before they call. Knowing who's on the phone before you check the caller ID. Having a dream about something that happens the next day. That's your natural remote viewing ability leaking through when your conscious mind isn't getting in the way.

The problem is that most of us have been trained out of trusting this kind of information. We're told it's coincidence, imagination, or wishful thinking. So we dismiss it. We push it down. We learn to ignore the quiet voice that knows things it shouldn't know. Remote viewing is just the practice of turning that volume back up - but doing it in a way that's structured and verifiable, so you can actually see what's real and what's just your brain making stuff up.

Remote viewing isn't about being special. It's about paying attention to what you already know but were never taught to trust.

Why Your Brain Will Fight You On This

Here's the thing nobody tells you when you start learning remote viewing. Your brain is going to hate it. Not because it's hard in the way that calculus is hard, but because it challenges everything your brain thinks it knows about how reality works.

Your brain has spent your entire life building a model of the world based on sensory input. Eyes see things. Ears hear things. Skin feels things. That's the data stream your brain trusts. When you start remote viewing, you're asking your brain to accept information from a channel it doesn't recognize. And your brain's first response to that is to reject it, explain it away, or dress it up as something familiar so it doesn't have to deal with the weirdness.

This is why the first few times you try remote viewing, you'll probably get nothing. Or you'll get things that feel completely made up. Or you'll get one tiny detail that's correct buried under a mountain of stuff that's completely wrong. That's normal. That's your brain learning to open a door it's kept locked your whole life.

The most common trap people fall into is thinking they're "just guessing" or "making it up." You'll have a target - say, a location you've never seen - and you'll start getting impressions. A feeling of cold. The sense of something tall. A color that keeps popping into your head. And your analytical mind will immediately jump in and say, "That's not real. You're just imagining things. You have no way of knowing that."

Here's the trick. You don't need to know how you know. You just need to record what comes through. The "how" is not your job. Your job is to be a receiver, not an interpreter. The analysis comes later, when you compare your notes to the actual target. Trying to figure out if something is real while you're receiving it is like trying to fix a car engine while you're driving it. It doesn't work. You'll crash.

One of the best resources for understanding this process is Limitless Mind by Russell Targ, one of the original scientists behind the Stargate Project. See on Amazon (paid link) He breaks down exactly why the analytical mind is the enemy of remote viewing and how to work around it.

The Setup: What You Actually Need

You don't need a dark room with candles. You don't need crystals or special music. You don't need to meditate for an hour beforehand. Remote viewing works best when you're relaxed but alert, and you can achieve that state in about five minutes once you know what you're doing.

Here's what you do need:

  • A quiet space where you won't be interrupted for 20-30 minutes
  • Paper and pen (not a computer or phone - the screen light interferes with the process)
  • A target. This is the thing you're trying to perceive. For practice, you'll use a sealed envelope containing a photo or a description of a location
  • A willingness to be wrong. This is non-negotiable. If you need to be right, you'll block the information before it even arrives

The target is the most important piece. When you're learning, you want targets that are simple and concrete. A beach. A mountain. A building. A room. Something with clear physical characteristics that you can verify later. You don't want abstract concepts or emotions or "the meaning of life." You want something that has a shape, a color, a texture, a temperature.

You can create your own targets by having a friend put a picture in an envelope and seal it. Or you can use online target pools where other remote viewers have submitted targets. The key is that you don't know what the target is. Your conscious mind needs to be blind. That's the whole point. You're training your deeper mind to reach for information that your conscious mind has no access to.

The Step-by-Step Protocol (This Is Where It Gets Real)

Okay, let's get into the actual process. This is the basic protocol used in the Stargate Project. It's called Coordinate Remote Viewing, or CRV. I'm going to give you the simplified version that you can actually use without a training manual the size of a phone book.

Step 1: Get Into the Right State

Sit comfortably. Take three slow breaths. Don't try to empty your mind - that's not the goal. Just let your attention settle into your body. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel the weight of your body in the chair. Notice any tension and let it soften. You're not trying to go into a trance. You're just trying to get out of your own way.

This takes about two minutes. Don't rush it. If you skip this step, you'll spend the whole session fighting your own thoughts.

Step 2: Declare Your Intention

Hold the sealed envelope in your hands. Say (out loud or silently): "I intend to perceive the target contained in this envelope." That's it. No grand pronouncements. No begging the universe. Just a clear statement of what you're doing. Your deeper mind needs to know what you're asking for.

Step 3: Record Your Initial Impressions

Open your eyes. Write down whatever comes to mind first. Don't censor it. Don't judge it. Don't try to make it make sense. If you get "cold," write it down. If you get "red," write it down. If you get "something about my grandmother's kitchen," write it down. Even if it seems completely random or stupid. Especially if it seems completely random or stupid.

This is the stage where most people fail. They get an impression, their brain immediately says "that's dumb," and they discard it. Don't discard anything. Every impression is data, even the ones that turn out to be wrong. You need to see the pattern of what's real and what's noise, and you can't see that pattern if you're throwing away half the data.

Write in bullet points. Short phrases. Don't try to form complete sentences. Sentences engage your analytical mind, and that's the part that makes stuff up. Bullet points let the information come through without your brain trying to organize it into a story.

Step 4: Move Into Sensory Impressions

Once you've gotten the initial burst of impressions, close your eyes again. This time, ask yourself: "What do I sense about this target?" Not what you see, but what you sense. Temperature. Texture. Weight. Sound. Smell. The feeling of the space. Is it open or enclosed? Is it natural or man-made? Is there movement or stillness?

This is where remote viewing gets interesting. Your visual imagination is very easy to fool. But your body's sensory memory is much harder to trick. If you get a strong feeling of dampness and cold, that's probably real. If you get a detailed visual image of a specific building with specific architectural details, that's probably your imagination filling in the gaps.

Trust the sensations more than the pictures. The pictures are often wrong. The sensations are usually right.

Practical Intuition by Laura Day has excellent exercises for developing this kind of sensory awareness. See on Amazon (paid link) Her approach is very grounded and practical - no fluff, just direct training.

Step 5: Draw What You're Getting

Get a fresh piece of paper. Don't try to draw a picture of the target. You're not an artist, and that's not the point. Draw the shapes and forms you're sensing. A line that feels vertical. A curve. An angle. A sense of mass or emptiness. Use simple marks. Don't try to make it look like anything. Just let your hand move based on what you're sensing.

This bypasses your analytical mind completely. Your hand knows things your conscious brain doesn't. When you're stuck, drawing is the best way to get unstuck. It doesn't have to make sense. It just has to be honest.

Step 6: Ask Specific Questions

Once you've gotten the basic impressions and drawings, you can start asking more specific questions. But here's the key: ask about sensory qualities, not about identity. Don't ask "Is this a building?" Ask "Does this have hard surfaces? Is it tall? Is there an opening?" Let the answers come without trying to name what the target is.

Naming is the enemy of accuracy. The moment you say "this is a bridge," your brain will start pulling up everything it knows about bridges and layering it over the actual target. You'll get a generic bridge instead of the specific bridge that's actually in the envelope. Stay in the sensory realm as long as you can.

Step 7: Close the Session

When you feel like you're done - usually after 20-30 minutes - thank yourself for the information. Put your notes aside. Do not look at the target yet. Wait at least an hour, preferably a day, before you open the envelope. This gives your analytical mind time to settle down so you can look at your notes with fresh eyes.

When you do open the envelope, compare your notes to the target. Don't beat yourself up for what you got wrong. Celebrate what you got right. Even if it's one small thing out of twenty, that's a success. That's your deeper mind reaching through the noise and grabbing something real. The accuracy will improve with practice.

Why You'll Get It Wrong (And Why That's Fine)

Let me be straight with you. Your first dozen sessions are going to be mostly wrong. You'll get a few hits - maybe a color that matches, or a shape that's close - but most of what you record will be noise. That's not failure. That's learning.

Think about it like learning to hear a conversation in a noisy room. At first, you just hear a wall of sound. But as you focus, you start to pick out a voice here, a word there. Eventually, you can follow the whole conversation. Remote viewing is the same. The noise is your own mind - your memories, your expectations, your fears, your desires. The signal is the actual target information. Learning to separate them takes time.

The biggest mistake beginners make is quitting after a few sessions because they think they're "not psychic." You're not supposed to be good at this right away. You're supposed to be clumsy and confused and mostly wrong. That's how learning works. The people who get good at remote viewing are the ones who keep showing up even when it feels like nothing is happening.

There's a book called Practical Intuition for Success that has a great chapter on dealing with the frustration of early practice. See on Amazon (paid link) It's not specifically about remote viewing, but the principles of developing any intuitive skill are the same.

Common Problems And How To Fix Them

Problem: You're getting nothing but blankness

This usually means you're trying too hard. Remote viewing is not an effortful activity. It's receptive, not active. The harder you try to "see" something, the more you'll block the information. Back off. Relax your focus. Let the impressions come to you instead of reaching for them. Sometimes the best thing to do is take a break and come back later.

Problem: You're getting detailed images that are completely wrong

This is your analytical mind taking over. It doesn't like the ambiguity of real remote viewing, so it fills in the gaps with stuff from your memory. The fix is to stay in sensory impressions - temperature, texture, weight, space - and avoid visual imagery. If you get a strong visual, write it down but don't trust it. See if the sensory impressions underneath it are more accurate.

Problem: You're getting accurate information but you don't trust it

This is the most common problem, and it's also the most frustrating. You get a hit - you write "cold" and the target turns out to be a glacier - but your brain says "that was just a lucky guess." Maybe it was. But maybe it wasn't. The only way to know is to keep practicing and look for patterns. If you consistently get sensory impressions that match the target, you're not lucky. You're doing it right. Start trusting the process, even when your brain tells you not to.

Problem: You're getting bored or frustrated

Switch up your targets. Use locations that actually interest you. Or take a break for a few days. Remote viewing should feel like exploration, not homework. If it feels like a chore, you're doing it wrong. The best remote viewers are the ones who stay curious and playful.

For a deeper dive into the psychology of developing these skills, Becoming Supernatural by Dr. Joe Dispenza covers the brain states involved in accessing non-local information. See on Amazon (paid link) His work on how the brain creates reality is directly relevant to understanding why remote viewing works.

How To Know You're Making Progress

Progress in remote viewing doesn't look like what you'd expect. It's not that you start getting every target perfectly. It's that the ratio of signal to noise improves. You get more hits per session. You start to recognize the feeling of real information versus the feeling of your imagination. You trust your first impressions more and your second-guessing less.

You'll also start noticing that your remote viewing sessions affect the rest of your life. You'll have more intuitive hits in everyday situations. You'll know who's calling before you check the phone. You'll get hunches about people and situations that turn out to be accurate. That's the skill generalizing. You're not just learning to perceive targets in envelopes - you're learning to perceive information that's always been there, but that you were trained to ignore.

Keep a journal of your sessions. Date them. Note what worked and what didn't. Look for patterns. Maybe you're better with natural targets than man-made ones. Maybe you get more accurate information in the morning than at night. Maybe your drawings are more accurate than your words. Learn your own style. Everyone's remote viewing process is slightly different. The protocol is a starting point, not a prison.

The Real Point Of All This

Here's what nobody tells you about remote viewing. The point isn't to become some kind of psychic spy who can describe a random building in Moscow. The point is to prove to yourself that your consciousness is not confined to your skull. That you can know things you shouldn't be able to know. That the world is bigger and stranger and more connected than your materialist culture told you it was.

That's the real gift of this practice. Not the ability to describe targets, but the lived experience that your mind reaches beyond your body. That you are not just a brain in a bone box. That information flows through you in ways that science is only beginning to understand.

And once you know that - really know it, from your own experience, not from reading about it - everything changes. You stop being afraid of the unknown. You start trusting your own knowing. You become more sensitive, more aware, more alive. That's the bright wound I keep talking about. The wound of knowing too much, feeling too much, sensing too much. It's not a curse. It's a gift you're learning to unwrap.

Start with the protocol I gave you. Do it every day for a week. Even if you get nothing. Even if it feels stupid. Show up, do the steps, record your impressions. After seven days, look back at

author: Kalesh
